perturbation.original_text: Consultant represents as follows: (a) Consultant is not subject to any other agreement that Consultant will violate by signing this Agreement; (b) Consultant has and shall continue to have the knowledge, experience, qualifications and required skill to perform, and shall perform, the Services in a professional manner; (c) Consultant to perform the Services in accordance with all Applicable Law; and (d) During the term of this Agreement, Consultant will not, directly or indirectly (whether for compensation or without compensation) engage in or provide consulting services, or enter into any agreement either written or oral, that would present a material conflict with any of the provisions of this Agreement, or would preclude Consultant from complying with the terms and conditions hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Consultant represents as follows: (a) Consultant will make reasonable efforts to ensure that signing this Agreement does not violate any other agreement; (b) Consultant believes they possess the knowledge, experience, and qualifications to perform the Services adequately; (c) Consultant will endeavor to perform the Services in general accordance with Applicable Law; and (d) During the term of this Agreement, Consultant will try to avoid engaging in activities that materially conflict with this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text contains clear representations regarding the consultant's obligations and capabilities. The modified text weakens these obligations by introducing subjective qualifiers such as 'reasonable efforts,' 'believes,' 'adequately,' 'endeavor,' and 'try to avoid.' This creates ambiguity regarding the consultant's commitment to compliance and performance, potentially making it difficult to enforce these provisions.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modifications weaken the consultant's obligations to a point where they might not meet the standard of fair and honest dealing, which California Business and Professions Code § 17200 prohibits. By using terms like 'reasonable efforts' and 'believes,' the agreement introduces a level of subjectivity that could allow the consultant to avoid fulfilling their responsibilities, potentially constituting an unfair business practice. The 'endeavor to perform' clause could fall short of the law's implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 17200 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 5. Enforcement [17200 - 17210] ( Chapter 5 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 299. ) 17200. As used in this chapter, unfair competition shall mean and include any unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice and unfair, deceptive, untrue or misleading advertising and any act prohibited by Chapter 1 (commencing with Section 17500) of Part 3 of Division 7 of the Business and Professions Code. (Amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 430, Sec. 2. Effective January 1, 1993.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguities that weaken the consultant's obligations, potentially conflicting with the standard of fair dealing under California Business and Professions Code § 17200. The original text uses definitive language, while the changed text uses terms like 'reasonable efforts,' 'believes,' and 'endeavor to perform,' which introduce subjectivity and could allow the consultant to avoid fulfilling their responsibilities. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines unfair competition under BPC 17200 as 'any unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice'. By weakening the obligations, the modified contract arguably introduces unfairness, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Aduro shall be the sole and exclusive owner of, and Consultant hereby assigns to Aduro, any and all writings, documents, work product, inventions, developments, improvements, discoveries, know-how, processes, chemical entities, compounds, plans, memoranda, tests, research, designs, specifications, models and data that Consultant makes, conceives, discovers or develops, either solely or jointly with any other person in performance of the Services (collectively, "Work Product").
perturbation.changed_text: Aduro shall generally own the Work Product, but Consultant retains rights to use general knowledge and expertise gained during the performance of the Services. The precise scope of Aduro's ownership will be determined on a case-by-case basis, considering the specific circumstances of each invention, development, or discovery.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly assigns all Work Product to Aduro. The modified text introduces ambiguity by stating that Aduro 'generally' owns the Work Product and allowing the Consultant to retain rights to use 'general knowledge and expertise.' It also introduces a case-by-case determination of ownership, which is vague and could lead to disputes. This conflicts with established intellectual property law and the principle of clear assignment of rights.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Labor Code § 2860 states that everything an employee acquires by virtue of their employment, except compensation, belongs to the employer. The modification allowing the consultant to retain rights to use 'general knowledge and expertise,' and the case-by-case determination of ownership creates ambiguity that potentially contradicts this law. This ambiguity allows the consultant to claim ownership of or rights to use work product directly related to their services, which should belong solely to Aduro under California Labor Code.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, LAB 2860 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Labor Code - LAB DIVISION 3. EMPLOYMENT RELATIONS [2700 - 3122.4] ( Division 3 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) CHAPTER 2. Employer and Employee [2750 - 2930] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) ARTICLE 3. Obligations of Employee [2850 - 2866] ( Article 3 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) 2860. Everything which an employee acquires by virtue of his employment, except the compensation which is due to him from his employer, belongs to the employer, whether acquired lawfully or unlawfully, or during or after the expiration of the term of his employment. (Enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts California Labor Code § 2860. The original contract assigns all 'Work Product' to Aduro. The changed text introduces ambiguity by stating Aduro 'generally' owns the Work Product and allowing the Consultant to retain rights to use 'general knowledge and expertise,' and introduces a case-by-case determination of ownership. California Labor Code § 2860 states, 'Everything which an employee acquires by virtue of his employment... belongs to the employer...' By allowing the consultant to retain rights, the changed text contradicts the law, which clearly states that what the employee acquires through employment belongs to the employer. The ambiguity introduced directly undermines the employer's ownership rights as defined by the law.

perturbation.original_text: Consultant shall keep all Confidential Information confidential, and Consultant shall not disclose, disseminate, publish, reproduce or use Confidential Information except to perform the Services.
perturbation.changed_text: Consultant will endeavor to keep Confidential Information confidential, and will generally refrain from disclosing, disseminating, publishing, reproducing, or using Confidential Information, except as necessary.
perturbation.explanation: The original text imposes a strict obligation to maintain confidentiality. The modified text weakens this obligation by replacing 'shall' with 'will endeavor' and 'generally refrain,' making the confidentiality requirement less stringent and more discretionary. The addition of 'except as necessary' is also vague and introduces ambiguity as to what constitutes 'necessary' use or disclosure of Confidential Information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Cal. Civ. Code § 3426)
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) provides legal remedies for the misappropriation of trade secrets. By weakening the confidentiality obligation to 'will endeavor' and allowing disclosure 'except as necessary,' the modified clause creates a loophole. This makes it more difficult for Aduro to prove that the consultant violated the agreement and misappropriated trade secrets if the consultant claims they were merely 'endeavoring' to maintain confidentiality or that the disclosure was 'necessary,' thereby undermining the protections afforded by CUTSA. CUTSA requires a duty to maintain secrecy which is not fulfilled in the modified language.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens the confidentiality obligation, creating a legal ambiguity. The original text states 'Consultant shall keep' which implies a strict obligation. The modified text changes this to 'Consultant will endeavor to keep' and 'will generally refrain,' making the obligation less strict and introduces uncertainty. This undermines the trade secret protection as it makes enforcement difficult if the consultant only 'endeavors' to maintain confidentiality. The law snippet 1, referencing the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) within the Civil Code, indicates a legal framework for trade secret protection. The change introduces ambiguity which contradicts the requirement for a clear duty to maintain secrecy as described in the Law Explanation.
